title: Krantz cake
subtitle: Israeli twisted loaf with a chocolate and cinnamon filling
excerpt: An Israeli celebration loaf made from enriched dough spread with a chocolate and cinnamon filling, rolled tightly, sliced lengthways, and twisted into a plait before being baked in a loaf tin. The slicing and twisting exposes the filling layers and creates the dramatic spiral cross-section. Brushed with a sugar syrup after baking for a glossy, sticky finish. Sometimes called chocolate babka, though the Israeli version uses a sugar syrup glaze where the Polish version does not.
sourceNotes: Krantz cake (also written krantz-kugel or chocolate babka) is documented in Ashkenazi and Israeli baking literature from the late nineteenth century as an enriched dough loaf with a sweet filling, shaped by rolling, slicing, and twisting. The basic format appears in Jennie Grossinger, The Art of Jewish Cooking (1958, now out of copyright under US duration rules applied to works published before 1964). The chocolate-cinnamon filling combination is the standard Israeli version, described in Israeli domestic baking guides from the 1950s onward.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze on the day of baking, whole or sliced. Defrost overnight at room temperature. Warm in a 160°C oven for 10 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The dough can be made, shaped, and refrigerated overnight before baking. Bring to room temperature for 1 hour and bake as directed.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 160°C. A moderate temperature gives the thick loaf time to cook through without the crust darkening too fast.

recipeTools.notes: With dough hook, for the enriched dough.

glossaryTerms.definition: The shaping method for krantz cake: roll the filled dough into a tight log, then cut the log lengthways down the middle to expose the filling layers. Take the two halves and twist them around each other with the cut sides facing up throughout, so the layers remain visible. Lift the twisted rope into the loaf tin, fitting it in lengthways. The twist opens and expands during proving and baking, creating the characteristic layered cross-section. Work quickly once the filling is on; if the dough warms and the filling softens, it is harder to keep the layers distinct.

content.text: Krantz cake is more straightforward than it looks. The enriched dough is the same as any soft loaf; the drama comes from the filling and

. Chilling the rolled, filled log for 30 minutes before slicing makes the cut cleaner and keeps the layers more defined as you twist. The sugar syrup brushed on straight from the oven is what gives the top its sticky, mahogany glaze.

Combine flour, yeast, salt, and sugar in a large bowl. Add the warm milk and eggs and mix to a rough dough. Knead for 8 minutes until smooth. Work in the soft butter gradually and knead for a further 5 minutes. Place in an oiled bowl, cover, and leave for 60 minutes until doubled.

Mix the filling ingredients together until smooth. Roll the dough on a lightly floured surface into a rectangle about 40 x 50 cm. Spread the filling evenly across the surface, leaving a 2 cm border at the far edge. Roll up tightly from the near edge into a log. Wrap in cling film and refrigerate for 30 minutes.

Oil a 900 g loaf tin. Remove the chilled log and cut it lengthways down the centre. Twist the two halves around each other, cut sides up, keeping the layers visible. Lift into the tin. Cover and prove for 45 minutes until the dough is level with or just above the rim. Heat the oven to 180°C conventional (160°C fan).

Bake for 32 to 38 minutes until deep brown and cooked through. While it bakes, dissolve the sugar in the water in a small saucepan over medium heat, bring to the boil, then remove from the heat. As soon as the loaf comes out of the oven, brush all over with the hot syrup. Leave in the tin for 15 minutes before turning out.
